<details><summary>Thanks and Notes</summary>
<p dir="auto">
</p><p dir="auto"><sup>If you have time, so read more with the book <code>Android System Programming - Porting, customizing, and debugging Android HAL</code> by Roger Ye  ---- First published: May 2017 - 464 pages</sup></p>
<p dir="auto"><sup>Thanks of the Android Source Code <a href="https://android.googlesource.com/platform/" rel="nofollow">AOSP</a> and to all developers ope source who help and update that.</sup></p>
<p dir="auto"><sup>Thanks to the <a href="https://4pda.to/forum/index.php?showforum=281" rel="nofollow">4pda forum</a> and everyone on this forum who contribute to the development in general for Custom Recovery, Custom ROMs/GSI and tools to use with device development.</sup></p>
<p dir="auto"><sup>Thank you very much to <a href="https://xdaforums.com/m/yuweng.4478781/" rel="nofollow">yuweng</a> because this guy is not only humble and generous, he was a forerunner for Mediatek devices to actually have Custom Recovery. I had the opportunity to communicate with him and it was great! This guy created tools and tutorials to make everything easier for newbies. Well, I didn't use it much because I prefer to do the Portability process manually. Among the tools, <a href="https://xdaforums.com/t/port-tool-carliv-touch-recovery-for-mediatek-devices.1825722/" rel="nofollow">[PORT] [TOOL] Carliv Touch Recovery for MediaTek</a>, <a href="https://xdaforums.com/t/easy-magic-philz-touch-installer-for-mediatek-devices-only-developers-only.2748169/" rel="nofollow">Easy Magic PhilZ Touch Installer for MediaTek</a> and <a href="https://xdaforums.com/t/easy-magic-twrp-installer-for-all-mediatek-devices-only-developers-only.2798257/" rel="nofollow">Easy Magic TWRP Installer for all MediaTek</a> stand out. Also, he wrote a great tutorial on <a href="https://xdaforums.com/t/guide-how-to-compile-philz-touch-6-recovery-for-arm-android-devices-using-bbqlinux.2862706/" rel="nofollow">how to compile PhilZ Touch 6</a> Custom Recovery. I also highlight his ability to help other users with the tutorial <a href="https://xdaforums.com/t/guide-how-to-unbrick-your-mediatek-mt65xx.1943442/" rel="nofollow">[GUIDE] How to 'unbrick' your Mediatek MT65xx</a> and <a href="https://xdaforums.com/t/tut-tool-mt65xx-rom-porting.2123239/" rel="nofollow">[TUT] [TOOL] MT65xx ROM Porting</a>, to add knowledge and develop in relation to the Android. I could still write many other things about him made, but it would go far beyond the subject.</sup></p>
<p dir="auto"><sup>Thanks to <a href="https://xdaforums.com/m/phil3759.4666971/" rel="nofollow">Phil3759</a> because he did a <a href="https://xdaforums.com/t/2015-10-09-cwm-6-0-5-1-philz-touch-6-59-0-libtouch_gui-1-42.2201860/" rel="nofollow">CWM/PhilZ Touch</a> Custom Recovery - very interesting and still needed today. I learned how to improve the PORT Custom Recovery process with this Custom Recovery.</sup></p>
<p dir="auto"><sup>Thanks to Carliv aka <a href="https://xdaforums.com/m/bluefirebird.5186178/" rel="nofollow">bluefirebird</a> because I learned more from him about compiling <a href="https://xdaforums.com/t/recovery-carliv-touch-recovery-v5-7-and-v6-6.3389290/" rel="nofollow">CTR.img</a> is better than exchanging files with PORT Custom Recovery process. Furthermore, this guy also developed <a href="https://xdaforums.com/t/eol-recovery-carlivtouchrecovery-v2-7-4-2-2-for-port-to-mtk-phones-eol.2471479/" rel="nofollow">CTR for Mediatek</a> devices. Well, you might know him more because of <a href="https://xdaforums.com/t/tool-utility-carliv-image-kitchen-for-android-unpack-repack-boot-recovery.3013658/" rel="nofollow">CarlivImageKitchen</a> that he developed.</sup></p>
<p dir="auto"><sup>Thanks of the Source Code Custom Recovery to <a href="https://twrp.me/" rel="nofollow">@TeamWin</a> and to all the people who help update that, as well as the people who help Mediatek devices to have a more functional Custom Recovery.</sup></p>
<p dir="auto"><sup>Thanks of the Source Code Custom Recovery to <a href="https://wiki.orangefox.tech/en/home" rel="nofollow">@OrangeFox</a> and to all the people who help update that, as well as the people who help Mediatek devices to have a more functional Custom Recovery.</sup></p>
<p dir="auto"><sup>Thanks of the Source Code Custom Recovery to <a href="https://pitchblackrecovery.com/" rel="nofollow">@PitchBlack</a> and to all the people who help update that, as well as the people who help Mediatek devices to have a more functional Custom Recovery.</sup></p>
<p dir="auto"><sup>Thanks to @osm0sis for keeping <a href="https://github.com/osm0sis/Android-Image-Kitchen">AIK</a> always up to date for use.</sup></p>
<p dir="auto"><sup>Thanks to @SebaUbuntu for <a href="https://github.com/twrpdtgen/twrpdtgen">twrpdtgen</a>.</sup></p>
<p dir="auto"><sup>Thanks to all the people who left some <a href="https://github.com/search?q=device+mt6">Mediatek device tree repository</a> available so that other users can fork and not waste too much time creating a device tree from nothing.</sup></p>
<p dir="auto"><sup>How I write in gist: <a href="https://docs.github.com/pt/get-started/writing-on-github">Writing on github</a></sup></p>
<p dir="auto"><sup>How I compile img file with Action in github: <a href="https://gist.github.com/lopestom/d85ce99bb244d308c5e681db31018626">Using online resource to build custom recovery - TeamWin - Fox - SHRP - PBRP</a></sup></p>
<p dir="auto"><sup><a href="https://www.makeuseof.com/tag/whats-custom-recovery-exploring-cwm-twrp-friends/" rel="nofollow">What Is a Custom Android Recovery?</a></sup></p>
<p dir="auto"><sup><a href="https://www.engadget.com/2011-12-20-team-win-recovery-project-2-0-goes-gold-tells-your-volume-rocke.html?guccounter=1" rel="nofollow">Team Win Recovery Project 2.0 goes gold</a></sup></p>
<p dir="auto"><sup><a href="https://www.addictivetips.com/android/what-is-twrp-how-to-install-use-it-on-android-devices-guide/" rel="nofollow">What Is TWRP Recovery & How To Install & Use It On Android Devices [Guide]</a></sup></p>
<p dir="auto"></p>
</details>

      <blockquote>
<p dir="auto">How much can i pay you to make TWRP for tecno camon 19 neo?</p>
</blockquote>
<p dir="auto">For me, nothing: <a href="https://github.com/lopestom/twrp_device_tecno_CH6i">TWRP device tree for Tecno Camon 19 Neo (CH6i) - MT6768 - Helio G85 - A12</a><br>
Maybe this too: <a href="https://github.com/infinixs5lite/android_device_CH6i-TWRP">https://github.com/infinixs5lite/android_device_CH6i-TWRP</a></p>
<p dir="auto">The problem is about encrypt/decrypt of <a href="https://github.com/lopestom/twrp_device_ulefone_Power_Armor14_Pro?tab=readme-ov-file#whats-the-history">only 32MB with Android 12</a>.</p>
<p dir="auto">You can fork any repository and compile your img file.</p>

        <div class="edit-comment-hide">

    <div class="comment-body markdown-body js-comment-body soft-wrap user-select-contain d-block">
      <blockquote>
<p dir="auto">Device: LG Stylo 6 which is also lge mdh50lm Arch: Arm64 Soc: mt6765 Android 10</p>
<p dir="auto">Stock Rom doesn't have recovery image or ability to use fastboot boot twrp.img. i have to use fastboot flash boot_a twrp.img to test. But no, stock Rom has vendor boot img and boot img but no recovery image. That's why I ran using option to give me ramdisk recovery img.</p>
<p dir="auto">Meta_init is in there only because twrpdtgen put it there when compiling in termux.</p>
<p dir="auto">I used 12 because I thought 10 deprecated meant it would not work.</p>
</blockquote>
<p dir="auto"><a class="user-mention notranslate" data-hovercard-type="user" data-hovercard-url="/users/LostOuija/hovercard" data-octo-click="hovercard-link-click" data-octo-dimensions="link_type:self" href="https://github.com/LostOuija" aria-keyshortcuts="Alt+ArrowUp">@LostOuija</a></p>
<blockquote>
<p dir="auto"><a class="user-mention notranslate" data-hovercard-type="user" data-hovercard-url="/users/lopestom/hovercard" data-octo-click="hovercard-link-click" data-octo-dimensions="link_type:self" href="https://github.com/lopestom" aria-keyshortcuts="Alt+ArrowUp">@lopestom</a> I made several changes throughout the entire tree and after many failed attempts have successfully built the twrp for this device that many have struggled to succeed even rooting. Mind glancing over some of my finalized work? <a href="https://github.com/LostOuija/twrp_device_lge_mdh50lm/releases/tag/10264386085">https://github.com/LostOuija/twrp_device_lge_mdh50lm/releases/tag/10264386085</a></p>
</blockquote>
<p dir="auto">I see very files not used but normal if DT not need.</p>
<blockquote>
<p dir="auto">Also, as a little side note to help understand, my device is a/b partition scheme and in the stock Rom dump there is no recovery.img at all. Using fastboot boot TWRP.img does not work, nor does fastboot flash recovery. So board bootimg partition and recovery partition is the same size because the recovery is built in the boot img, so they would be the same size.</p>
</blockquote>
<p dir="auto">I push your comments for here because you can read and answer better. So with that you look normal instructions and directions to your build: <code class="notranslate">Build: twrp-11</code><br>
like I suggested in <a href="https://gist.github.com/lopestom/a5e6b690028cedd47d7e648a1035b358?permalink_comment_id=5133556#gistcomment-5133556">https://gist.github.com/lopestom/a5e6b690028cedd47d7e648a1035b358?permalink_comment_id=5133556#gistcomment-5133556</a></p>
<p dir="auto">In the twrp-11+ and pure kernel Android 11+ the devices can't boot TWRP with command <code class="notranslate">fastboot boot TWRP.img</code> so only install directly with <code class="notranslate">fastboot flash boot TWRP.img</code>.</p>
    </div>
  </task-lists>
  
</div>

    <div class="comment-body markdown-body js-comment-body soft-wrap user-select-contain d-block">
      <p dir="auto">I am looking to hire someone to solve some software issues for me. I thought I could do it myself, but ultimately this project has proved to be much more difficult than I anticipated. The reasons for this primarily are that I didn't know as much regarding low level Android stuff as I thought I did. Mostly it has to do with some specific MediaTek issues and not being able to find the source code or information, and Ultimately if I had that I could probably work through it eventually, but as it is I would really just rather hire somebody to take care of it so it is done right.</p>
<p dir="auto">I really like retro devices, kind of like taking an old Atari 2600 and put a raspberry Pi compute module 5 in it and playing retro games on it. So my most recent project is along that line.  It has 2 GB of PCDDR3 RAM and 32 GB of eMMC storage, which is running at 32 bits. This is a pretty underwhelming tablet, but it's got a nice screen and unless you're trying to do a whole lot on it actually runs pretty well. I'm mostly a hardware guy so the first thing I did was promptly upgrade the eMMC to 256 GB, an increase the RAM from 2 GB to 4 GB (the extra 2gb of RAM not being recognized because this is hard coded in the device). What I didn't know was that the software that comes with it is a bastardized version of Android 8.1 and it just sucks.  For me, rooting the tablet wasn't all that difficult, so it works just not as well as it could, but fixing the boot loaders and such has proven to be beyond my ability. The possibility of using bootloaders or preloader from another device seems remote since this device uses DDR3L (PCDDR3) rather than the more common LPDDR3 most devices of this class use, so substitute preloaders simply fail at the RAM initialization process. This is a nice little tablet I just want it to have better performance. I decrypted the user partition and saw a huge increase in performance, so overall I would like to take all of the encryption, secure boot, anything like that off of this version of Android, and if possible create a TWRP style recovery. Not only does it not need to be secure, I don't want it secure, I wanted as open and easy to operate as possible.</p>
<p dir="auto">So, what I would like are two things.</p>
<p dir="auto">1.) New (TWRP if possible) preloaders, bootloader's, and LK, LK2 for the Android 8.1 software I have.</p>
<p dir="auto">2.) A completely new versions of Android that is 64 bit, addresses the full 4gb of RAM, and is at least Android 9 or higher.</p>
<p dir="auto">I don't have any problem paying someone for their time, I just want it done right, and doing it right has proven to be beyond my own abilities. The hardware is right, I just need the software done.</p>
    </div>
  </task-lists>
  
</div>

    <div class="comment-body markdown-body js-comment-body soft-wrap user-select-contain d-block">
      <blockquote>
<p dir="auto">What I didn't know was that the software that comes with it is a bastardized version of Android 8.1 and it just sucks. For me, rooting the tablet wasn't all that difficult, so it works just not as well as it could, but fixing the boot loaders and such has proven to be beyond my ability. The possibility of using bootloaders or preloader from another device seems remote since this device uses DDR3L (PCDDR3) rather than the more common LPDDR3 most devices of this class use, so substitute preloaders simply fail at the RAM initialization process.</p>
</blockquote>
<p dir="auto">I understand what you want.<br>
But even if it is a Mediatek or a Qualcomm, the source code issues can really be a headache, especially regarding the proprietary files (which the company is not required to share and are not provided by GPL). Yes, even Qualcomm can block this. However, since it is Android 8.1, it may be possible to find something or drivers (proprietary files) that are already shared or that need small changes (only a great programmer/developer knows how to adapt this to the hardware). What is bad is the part of being 32-bit, something basically obsolete nowadays and not having performance (even with 8GB RAM and 256GB ROM). That is my humble opinion. Each one does what they want.....</p>
<p dir="auto">However, being an A8.1 and having the possibility of installing a GSI (not being a version higher than A13 - I think), would already improve your software/OS experience on the device. Furthermore, it would be a better decision than involve Android hardware and architecture improvements in a device that may not be worth the time and money.</p>
<p dir="auto">Have you experimented any GSI?</p>
<blockquote>
<p dir="auto">So, what I would like are two things.</p>
<p dir="auto">1.) New (TWRP if possible) preloaders, bootloader's, and LK, LK2 for the Android 8.1 software I have.</p>
</blockquote>
<p dir="auto">This part about preloader, lk, lk2 and others is the great sadness of not having the COMPLETE source code. It may or may not be possible. The best person to say this is a very experienced developer who can read and answer in more detail.</p>
<p dir="auto">I know of tools that could do reverse engineering or even create a new lk, but that would require experience and information.</p>
<blockquote>
<p dir="auto">2.) A completely new versions of Android that is 64 bit, addresses the full 4gb of RAM, and is at least Android 9 or higher.</p>
</blockquote>
<p dir="auto">Moving to a 64-bit structure will take more than time, since it would be necessary to build the entire kernel and OS files to run the A8.1 or A9 and possible updates for the A10,.... This idea is beyond what one can imagine, since it will require time and obviously the COMPLETE source code for such a feat. So I would give up on it and buy another device.</p>
<p dir="auto">Given everything you wrote, except for the physical part of the device, you want everything new and again (my opinion) I tell you, it is not worth the time and money to redo something for a new structure and version of Android.</p>
    </div>
  </task-lists>
  
</div>

            <a class="author Link--primary text-bold css-overflow-wrap-anywhere " show_full_name="false" data-hovercard-type="user" data-hovercard-url="/users/shrekanogre292/hovercard" data-octo-click="hovercard-link-click" data-octo-dimensions="link_type:self" href="/shrekanogre292" aria-keyshortcuts="Alt+ArrowUp">shrekanogre292</a>

        <a href="/lopestom/c4a2648958db5c3db03d32033a3583cd?permalink_comment_id=5488470#gistcomment-5488470" id="gistcomment-5488470-permalink" class="Link--secondary js-timestamp"><relative-time datetime="2025-03-12T18:09:02Z" class="no-wrap" title="Mar 12, 2025, 6:09 PM UTC">Mar 12, 2025</relative-time></a>

        <div class="edit-comment-hide">
  <task-lists disabled="" sortable="">
    <div class="comment-body markdown-body js-comment-body soft-wrap user-select-contain d-block">
      <p dir="auto">So, I understand what you are saying and I have considered all this prior to asking what I originally asked.  Let me address these one at a time.</p>
<p dir="auto">1.) it's not worth the money.</p>
<p dir="auto">A.) retro projects never are.  My 65 Corvette wasn't either, but despite spending more on the old one that it would have cost for a new one, it's worth it TO ME.</p>
<p dir="auto">2.) the GSI</p>
<p dir="auto">A.) I already have a version of LineageOS running on it, albeit at 32 bit, and I'm pretty sure that there is a way to port something that will work.  Yes, I'm aware that it'll take an expert, that's why I'm looking for one.  The biggest issues are the preloader, and kernels, which I will discuss shortly.</p>
<p dir="auto">3.) Android 9 / 64 bit structure</p>
<p dir="auto">A.) since this is a MediaTek chip and a common one, there are numerous tablets and phones and such out there that have virtually identical hardware specs that could be used as a basis for this upgrade.  Yes, the preloader and kernels will have to be reverse engineered for this specific device, but after that, the rest would be a modification of an existing image set.  Again, I realize that building or reverse engineering preloader and kernels requires an expert, but I also know it can be done.</p>
<p dir="auto">4.) Android 8.1</p>
<p dir="auto">A.) my experience with this has been that 8.1 is used on low RAM devices in 32 bit mode to accommodate the fact that it lacks hardware resources.  There are now lots of devices that run 64 bit OS on 1.5gb RAM just fine, and even more than run with 2 to 4gb of RAM.  The reason for upgrading the RAM to 4gb was because that's what the SoC will support, and other tablets use 4gb with this same SoC.  The main difference being these tabs use LPDDR3 rather than PCDDR3 so the preloader fails that RAM initialization test.  If I can get past this I think it very likely that rest of the image will work as the remainder of the hardware is identical in every way except memory type.</p>
<p dir="auto">5.) give up one it and get another device</p>
<p dir="auto">A.) in the end, I can use the device the way it is with what I've done, it's a rooted WORKING device, and while I'm not a fan of Oreo it gets the job done and the device is functional.  I just think it can be better.  I think it can be better under Oreo if we can utilize the extra RAM I have added, because the 2gb that it currently has is right on the verge of being enough and the cached processes slow down when swapping starts.  I think the installation of the software without all the security stuff and any type of encryption will dramatically improve performance (I've already seen a huge boost simply by removing the user partition encryption)</p>
<p dir="auto">To move to another Android version like Pie or newer should not be terribly difficult as the hardware is common and not exotic at all, and I have a working base OS to draw from, albeit it's 8.1</p>
<p dir="auto">In summary, I realize it will be difficult, but people do this sort of thing all the time, I just need to find someone who'd like to tackle it and earn some extra cash doing it.  IMO the preloader and kernels will be the bottleneck and once those are done, the rest should fall into place.  This is the situation for me, if those can get done, I'm sure I could do the rest over time, I'm just not capable of decompiling, altering, then recompiling these binaries, and just need to find someone who is.</p>
<p dir="auto">So, I'd like to talk with anyone who'd be interested in helping.</p>
    </div>
  </task-lists>
  
</div>

      <blockquote>
<p dir="auto"><a href="https://gist.github.com/lopestom/c4a2648958db5c3db03d32033a3583cd#33-beanpod-microtrust">Ref</a></p>
<p dir="auto">How do I know all necessary blobs. Some mentioned are not present in my device. Like <code class="notranslate">teei_daemon_vfs.so</code>.</p>
</blockquote>
<p dir="auto">Many devices from the same companies may have the same encryption type, but it all depends on the kernel version and Android version.<br>
Therefore, the files I referenced as blobs are not contained in your device's firmware, so just ignore them and find the ones you need.</p>

<p dir="auto"><a href="https://github.com/TeamWin/android_bootable_recovery/blob/202eaed300ee430c7cf69621e93637cd18dc372d/Android.mk#L559">https://github.com/TeamWin/android_bootable_recovery/blob/202eaed300ee430c7cf69621e93637cd18dc372d/Android.mk#L559</a></p>
</blockquote>

Example: for TWRP with A12+ so <a href="https://github.com/search?q=repo%3ATeamWin%2Fandroid_bootable_recovery%20keystore2&type=code">android-12.1</a> <a href="https://github.com/TeamWin/android_bootable_recovery/blob/202eaed300ee430c7cf69621e93637cd18dc372d/etc/init/keystore2.rc#L10">https://github.com/TeamWin/android_bootable_recovery/blob/202eaed300ee430c7cf69621e93637cd18dc372d/etc/init/keystore2.rc#L10</a><br>
<a href="https://github.com/TeamWin/android_bootable_recovery/blob/202eaed300ee430c7cf69621e93637cd18dc372d/Android.mk#L559">https://github.com/TeamWin/android_bootable_recovery/blob/202eaed300ee430c7cf69621e93637cd18dc372d/Android.mk#L559</a></p>
</blockquote>
<p dir="auto">thanks, but you should add new guide, keystore2 require more libraries like libsql. and now my libcrypto suck, that hang on twrp/orangefox screen</p>
<p dir="auto">08-09 06:50:09.388 1390 1390 F linker : CANNOT LINK EXECUTABLE "/system/bin/keystore2": cannot locate symbol "hmacSha256" referenced by "/system/bin/keystore2"...</p>
<p dir="auto">i'm tried fix with:android.hardware.identity-support-lib.so and libkeystore2_crypto.so but that still have even more error</p>
</blockquote>
<p dir="auto">If you knew how much I "fought" for something related to keystore2 that my devices didn't need, then you should read more about TeamWin's source code related to TWRP.<br>
You can also read here something I tried to help an acquaintance with his DT for another user's device: <a href="https://github.com/SKOCHE/twrp_blackview_N6000/issues/1" data-hovercard-type="issue" data-hovercard-url="/SKOCHE/twrp_blackview_N6000/issues/1/hovercard" aria-keyshortcuts="Alt+ArrowUp">I can't start encryption</a></p>
<p dir="auto">Just to be clear, I only had one MTK device with A12 and needed keymint. But it uses <code class="notranslate">beanpod</code> and never gave any error with keystore2!</p>
<p dir="auto">But just so you know, I've already achieved decryption in the trustkernel with A12_14. Before September 5, 2024, when I started doing something for a friend who had a device with trustkernel, nothing was resolved or worked. So I waited patiently, as I don't have enough knowledge to change the TWRP source code. Tests were run every time TeamWin released updates. Only on January 18, 2025, was the entire decryption "solved." How was this done? Simple, nothing. Again, nothing was changed in the DT or its files.<br>
This reflects the waiting time for TeamWin updates to the <a href="https://github.com/search?q=repo%3ATeamWin%2Fandroid_bootable_recovery%20keystore2&type=code">TWRP source code</a>.</p>
<p dir="auto">Recently, I bought a new device with an A14. With the A12, my device has kernel 5.10.xxx, and my other A14 device has kernel 5.15.xxx, where the Android framework has changed (again!).<br>
Even so, decryption doesn't work on the A14. This is due to the new twrp-14/twrp-14.1 branch and the updates TeamWin is rolling out. So, once again, patience is the solution at the moment.</p>
<p dir="auto">So, if something needs updating regarding keystore2, you can change something in the TWRP source code and even "should"/can send commits to TeamWin. This is something you can do yourself and consider if you want to share any solutions. It's up to you.<br>
<a href="https://github.com/minimal-manifest-twrp/platform_manifest_twrp_aosp#submitting-patches">Submitting Patches</a><br>
<a href="https://gerrit.twrp.me/q/status:open+-is:wip" rel="nofollow">gerrit TeamWin</a></p>
    </div>
  </task-lists>
  
</div>

      commented 


        <a href="/lopestom/c4a2648958db5c3db03d32033a3583cd?permalink_comment_id=5714782#gistcomment-5714782" id="gistcomment-5714782-permalink" class="Link--secondary js-timestamp"><relative-time datetime="2025-08-10T22:02:24Z" class="no-wrap" title="Aug 10, 2025, 10:02 PM UTC">Aug 10, 2025</relative-time></a>

        <div class="edit-comment-hide">
  <task-lists disabled="" sortable="">
    <div class="comment-body markdown-body js-comment-body soft-wrap user-select-contain d-block">
      <p dir="auto">I saw a completely broken mess regarding keystore2, but it messes up TeeMcDaemon and other encryption/decryption binaries.<br>
No one needs to write about 5.10.209-android12-9, which should really be compiled with twrp-12.1, because the Android version won't matter, but rather the kernel version and the \vendor version it was built with.</p>
<p dir="auto">I compiled TWRP on two devices, Infinix and Tecno, with the v4 header and Trustonic. The biggest issue is that something may have changed, either by TeamWin or the company itself.</p>
<p dir="auto">But some flags weren't used as you're specifying in your DT. So some can help, or they can hinder. It's all a matter of testing and confirmation.</p>
<pre class="notranslate"><code class="notranslate">TW_NO_BIND_SYSTEM := true
TW_NO_LEGACY_PROPS := true
TW_PREPARE_DATA_MEDIA_EARLY := true
</code></pre>
<p dir="auto"><a href="https://github.com/lopestom/twrp_device_infinix_X676C/blob/b3fb1d700614b7800580b33b12765c74c0f7f6b8/BoardConfig.mk#L196">https://github.com/lopestom/twrp_device_infinix_X676C/blob/b3fb1d700614b7800580b33b12765c74c0f7f6b8/BoardConfig.mk#L196</a></p>
<pre class="notranslate"><code class="notranslate">TW_INCLUDE_FBE := true
</code></pre>
<p dir="auto"><a href="https://github.com/lopestom/twrp_device_tecno_LG8n/blob/23ddf02a30030cecab1389faecc267c223790b73/BoardConfig.mk#L196">https://github.com/lopestom/twrp_device_tecno_LG8n/blob/23ddf02a30030cecab1389faecc267c223790b73/BoardConfig.mk#L196</a></p>
<p dir="auto">Regarding keystore2, I wouldn't even consider using it, but that's directly linked to TWRP's SC. What it does and processes according to the keymaster version is another matter. Even on kernels 5.10.xxx with A12, A13, and even A14, I've used keymaster@4 and keymaster@4.1. Obviously, after that, they added the <code class="notranslate">TW_FORCE_KEYMASTER_VER := true</code> flag for a reason.</p>
<p dir="auto">Your init file is quite different from anything I know or have seen. Could this be interfering with decryption?<br>
<a href="https://github.com/lopestom/twrp_device_tecno_LG8n/blob/23ddf02a30030cecab1389faecc267c223790b73/recovery/root/init.recovery.mt6789.rc#L68-L79">https://github.com/lopestom/twrp_device_tecno_LG8n/blob/23ddf02a30030cecab1389faecc267c223790b73/recovery/root/init.recovery.mt6789.rc#L68-L79</a></p>
<p dir="auto">I don't see the <code class="notranslate">first_stage_ramdisk</code> folder.</p>
<blockquote>
<p dir="auto">Now i'm stuck at screen with hidl config mess:</p>
</blockquote>
<p dir="auto">Even for MT6789 or MT6768 or any MTnnnn Soc/Platform based on kernel 5.10.xxx you can have this reference for Tecno/Infinix devices:<br>
<a href="https://github.com/transsion-mt6789-recovery/twrp-device_transsion_mt6789-common/blob/023ade2ffb8fc77e14f0850dd332a8474da9c099/BoardConfigCommon.mk#L129">https://github.com/transsion-mt6789-recovery/twrp-device_transsion_mt6789-common/blob/023ade2ffb8fc77e14f0850dd332a8474da9c099/BoardConfigCommon.mk#L129</a><br>
<a href="https://github.com/transsion-mt6789-recovery/twrp-device_transsion_mt6789-common/blob/023ade2ffb8fc77e14f0850dd332a8474da9c099/system.prop#L17">https://github.com/transsion-mt6789-recovery/twrp-device_transsion_mt6789-common/blob/023ade2ffb8fc77e14f0850dd332a8474da9c099/system.prop#L17</a></p>
<blockquote>
<p dir="auto">that use wrong version (4.0 instead 4.1), I'm fixed it but i'm mistake to use git reset --hard and i'm doing it again. But i'm forgot how to fix, that will take hours</p>
</blockquote>
<p dir="auto">Since your device and DT are based on <code class="notranslate">android.hardware.keymaster@4.1-service.trustonic</code>, I don't see any direct relationship with keystore2. But this is an assumption based on my previous experiences, which may therefore change at any time, either in the Android + TWRP source code or by the company that manufactures the device itself.</p>
<p dir="auto"><a href="https://github.com/chunix64/android_device_tecno_TECNO-CK6n_old/blob/0198ed0178c640b47dd0d0ef9a46eb29d9bd369e/recovery/root/init.recovery.mt6768.rc#L107">https://github.com/chunix64/android_device_tecno_TECNO-CK6n_old/blob/0198ed0178c640b47dd0d0ef9a46eb29d9bd369e/recovery/root/init.recovery.mt6768.rc#L107</a></p>
<p dir="auto"><a href="https://github.com/transsion-mt6789-recovery/twrp-device_transsion_mt6789-common/blob/023ade2ffb8fc77e14f0850dd332a8474da9c099/recovery/root/init.recovery.mt6789.rc#L84-L85">https://github.com/transsion-mt6789-recovery/twrp-device_transsion_mt6789-common/blob/023ade2ffb8fc77e14f0850dd332a8474da9c099/recovery/root/init.recovery.mt6789.rc#L84-L85</a></p>
<p dir="auto">Why u not use the entire transsion-mt6789-recovery DT as a test?!</p>

</div>

    <div class="comment-body markdown-body js-comment-body soft-wrap user-select-contain d-block">
      <p dir="auto">I'm fixed, it might helpful for others. i'm just leave it here</p>
<h1 dir="auto">Fix for Keymaster 4.1 (Trustonic)</h1>
<h3 dir="auto">1. Service definition</h3>
<p dir="auto">Add an additional interface for backward compatibility:</p>
<pre lang="rc" class="notranslate"><code class="notranslate">service keymaster-4-1-trustonic /vendor/bin/hw/android.hardware.keymaster@4.1-service.trustonic
    interface android.hardware.keymaster@4.0::IKeymasterDevice default
    interface android.hardware.keymaster@4.1::IKeymasterDevice default
    user root
    group root system drmrpc
    disabled
    oneshot
    seclabel u:r:recovery:s0
</code></pre>
<hr>
<h3 dir="auto">2. Boot service requirement</h3>
<p dir="auto">You <strong>must</strong> use the boot service from stock:</p>
<pre class="notranslate"><code class="notranslate">android.hardware.boot@1.2-service
</code></pre>
<p dir="auto">Path:</p>
<pre class="notranslate"><code class="notranslate">recovery/root/vendor/bin/hw/android.hardware.boot@1.2-service
</code></pre>
<hr>
<h3 dir="auto">3. Required dependencies (in <code class="notranslate">recovery/root/vendor/lib64/</code>)</h3>
<pre class="notranslate"><code class="notranslate">android.hardware.boot@1.0.so
android.hardware.boot@1.1.so
android.hardware.boot@1.2.so
hw/android.hardware.boot@1.0-impl-1.2-mtkimpl.so
</code></pre>
<hr>
<h3 dir="auto">4. Cgroups dependencies (needed by boot-hal)</h3>
<p dir="auto">Copy from system image:</p>
<pre class="notranslate"><code class="notranslate">/system/etc/cgroups.json → recovery/root/etc/cgroups.json
</code></pre>
<hr>
<h3 dir="auto">5. Init dependencies</h3>
<p dir="auto">Add init service:</p>
<pre class="notranslate"><code class="notranslate">recovery/root/vendor/etc/init/android.hardware.boot@1.2-service.rc
</code></pre>
<p dir="auto">Then import and call it in <code class="notranslate">init.recovery.mtXXXX.rc</code> under <code class="notranslate">on fs</code>:</p>
<pre lang="rc" class="notranslate"><code class="notranslate">on fs
    write /proc/bootprof "INIT:fs"
    start boot-hal-1-2
</code></pre>
<hr>
<h3 dir="auto"><g-emoji class="g-emoji" alias="warning">⚠️</g-emoji> Important</h3>
<p dir="auto">If any of these files are missing:</p>
<ul dir="auto">
<li>You <strong>cannot boot to system</strong>.</li>
<li>You will only boot into recovery and hang at the splash screen.  (Hang at <a href="https://github.com/TeamWin/android_system_vold/blob/a164ba05c5fef288059774a776b2e6e1119957cf/MetadataCrypt.cpp#L90C21-L90C36">this line</a>)</li>
</ul>
<p dir="auto">Other:</p>
<ul dir="auto">
<li>No <code class="notranslate">*.ko</code> files in <code class="notranslate">/lib/modules</code> == Bootloop (Can not boot to both recovery and OS)</li>
<li>Source code not generate kernel module (*.ko) unless you have kernel source code. SO YOU NEED FIND COMMON MODULE FOR /recovery/root/lib/modules , IF YOU HAVE NOTHING --> bootloop. You can extract it from stock vendor_boot.img or boot.img</li>
<li>Make sure first_stage_ramdisk exist and not empty (especially is fstab.mtxxxx)</li>
<li>Booting to operating system requires <code class="notranslate">boot-hal</code> and <code class="notranslate">first_stage_ramdisk</code></li>
</ul>
    </div>
  </task-lists>
  
</div>

        <div class="edit-comment-hide">
  <task-lists disabled="" sortable="">
    <div class="comment-body markdown-body js-comment-body soft-wrap user-select-contain d-block">
      <blockquote>
<p dir="auto">I have working decryption in TrustKernel TWRP on a tablet that shipped with Android 14 and a 5.10.xx kernel. I don't know how this works, but it does :v</p>
<p dir="auto"><a href="https://github.com/rdndds/recovery-device_itel_P13001L">https://github.com/rdndds/recovery-device_itel_P13001L</a></p>
</blockquote>
<p dir="auto">Congratulations.</p>
<p dir="auto">Actually, I myself have no way of predicting when the trustkernel mode will work. But what I do know is that I managed to get something working since January 2025 with a <a href="https://4pda.to/forum/index.php?showtopic=1079401&view=findpost&p=134689517" rel="nofollow">Unihertz MT6895</a> with kernel 5.10.198-android12-9 and I noticed that it makes some difference.</p>
<details><summary>See the initial attempt date. - Click to open</summary>
<p dir="auto">
<a target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" href="https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/25868008/510143488-741fa7c8-93ed-4c0c-9eba-2707df5be1fe.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..XnBvFlCcXu8RmqhRG9rUEEikV_hmYthUwPGZryz9eTU"><img width="428" height="373" alt="PrintScreen_20251105_102035" src="https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/25868008/510143488-741fa7c8-93ed-4c0c-9eba-2707df5be1fe.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..XnBvFlCcXu8RmqhRG9rUEEikV_hmYthUwPGZryz9eTU" style="max-width: 100%; height: auto; max-height: 373px;"></a>
</p>
</details>
<p dir="auto">Even doing something for MT6789, sometimes it doesn't work and sometimes it works partially (be careful!). Even for other SoCs like <a href="https://4pda.to/forum/index.php?showtopic=1091352&view=findpost&p=135318264" rel="nofollow">Cubot MT6895</a> and with kernel 5.10.198-android12-9, there is a very specific detail. Even using the same settings as the previous Unihertz device, it didn't work the same way!</p>
<div class="markdown-alert markdown-alert-tip" dir="auto"><p class="markdown-alert-title" dir="auto"><svg class="octicon octicon-light-bulb mr-2" viewBox="0 0 16 16" version="1.1" width="16" height="16" aria-hidden="true"><path d="M8 1.5c-2.363 0-4 1.69-4 3.75 0 .984.424 1.625.984 2.304l.214.253c.223.264.47.556.673.848.284.411.537.896.621 1.49a.75.75 0 0 1-1.484.211c-.04-.282-.163-.547-.37-.847a8.456 8.456 0 0 0-.542-.68c-.084-.1-.173-.205-.268-.32C3.201 7.75 2.5 6.766 2.5 5.25 2.5 2.31 4.863 0 8 0s5.5 2.31 5.5 5.25c0 1.516-.701 2.5-1.328 3.259-.095.115-.184.22-.268.319-.207.245-.383.453-.541.681-.208.3-.33.565-.37.847a.751.751 0 0 1-1.485-.212c.084-.593.337-1.078.621-1.489.203-.292.45-.584.673-.848.075-.088.147-.173.213-.253.561-.679.985-1.32.985-2.304 0-2.06-1.637-3.75-4-3.75ZM5.75 12h4.5a.75.75 0 0 1 0 1.5h-4.5a.75.75 0 0 1 0-1.5ZM6 15.25a.75.75 0 0 1 .75-.75h2.5a.75.75 0 0 1 0 1.5h-2.5a.75.75 0 0 1-.75-.75Z"></path></svg>Tip</p><p dir="auto"><strong>Read the part about <code class="notranslate">Important Warning!!!</code>.</strong></p>
</div>
<p dir="auto">That's why I haven't yet opened my repositories for trustkernel with those kernel versions.</p>
<p dir="auto">But I've already made interesting contributions that might be helpful. HOWEVER, I still write and WARN about the importance of having backups of important files, especially the /<strong><code class="notranslate">persist</code></strong> partition.<br>
<a href="https://github.com/lopestom/device_ulefone_Power_Armor_18/tree/twrp-12.1">device_ulefone_Power_Armor_18</a><br>
<a href="https://github.com/unihertz-jelly-max/twrp_device_unihertz_jmax/tree/master">twrp_device_unihertz_jmax</a></p>
<p dir="auto">In general, your recovery-device_itel_P13001L repository doesn't have anything new. This is because we all depend on the "updated/improved" TeamWin source code for custom recoveries to work properly (it all depends on the situation!).<br>
It's also important to point out that you didn't do anything new, as the author <a class="user-mention notranslate" data-hovercard-type="user" data-hovercard-url="/users/ADeadTrousers/hovercard" data-octo-click="hovercard-link-click" data-octo-dimensions="link_type:self" href="https://github.com/ADeadTrousers" aria-keyshortcuts="Alt+ArrowUp">@ADeadTrousers</a> mentioned in <a href="https://gist.github.com/lopestom/c4a2648958db5c3db03d32033a3583cd#32-trustkernel">this gist</a>.</p>
<p dir="auto">In any case, the important thing is that it works 🎉 and that you're happy with it 😃.</p>
    </div>
  </task-lists>
  
</div>
